query: association between LY6K overexpression and prognosis in non-small cell lung cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A combined play of genetic or epigenetic factors also controls metastatic spread. Smoking exposure correlates to the activation of the ubiquitin-chemokine receptor type 4 (CXCR4) pathway, high tissue levels of E-selectin, activation of the nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF_B) signaling in pneumocytes, increased chemokine ligand 2 (CCL2) expression and macrophage infiltration in the lung microenvironment. This chemokine secretion by alveolar cells recruits neutrophils, which synthesize arachidonate 5-lipoxygenase (ALOX5)-dependent leukotriene. Leukotriene promotes the survival and proliferation of leukotriene B4-expressing metastatic clones. Neutrophils also secrete cathepsin G and elastases, which further facilitate metastatic growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology -- Intrinsic Cell Determinants
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic cells utilize cell-autonomous traits that facilitate homing and survival by altering the following genes: Gene of Rous sarcoma virus (SRC) tyrosine kinase signaling P38 and extracellular signal-regulated kinase-1 (ERK) mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAP) kinase signaling pathways

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology -- Genetic Factors Predisposing Cancers to Metastasis
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitor of cell differentiation 1 (ID1), matrix metalloproteinase 1 (MMP1), chemokine CXC motif ligand 1 (CXCL1), prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase (PTGS2), vascular cell adhesion molecule-1 (VCAM1), and epiregulin (EREG) are the genes that promote lung metastasis in animal models carrying breast carcinoma. ID1 promotes the formation of lung metastases by itself in animal models and expresses significantly in samples from breast cancer patients with lung metastases. Its activation promotes breast cancer dissemination by modulating S100A9 expression. Analysis of human cancer samples has shown that comparing lung and non-lung metastases from breast cancer identified 21 differentially expressed genes. These genes mainly encode adhesion molecules, which result in cell-to-cell interactions and thus facilitate lung colonization. Among them are integrins (ITGB8), cadherins (CDH3), desmosomal proteins (DSC2), and focal adhesion molecules (FERMT1).

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchoalveolar Cancer (Archived) -- Histopathology -- Lepidic predominant adenocarcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Naito et al looked at the microenvironmental changes in the transition of adenocarcinoma in situ to lepidic predominant invasive adenocarcinoma of the lung. During the progression from the adenocarcinoma in situ to lepidic predominant adenocarcinoma, expression of laminin 5 and ezrin in the invasive component was significantly increased. Tumor-promoting stromal cells were also expressed in higher proportions. High expression of ezrin is associated with lymphovascular invasion and poorer prognosis of LPA. It can thus be explored as a marker for the metastatic potential for these groups of neoplasms. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology -- Intrinsic Cell Determinants
retrieved_docs.content: SSeCKS (scaffold protein src-suppressed C-kinase substrate complex) dysregulation occurs in lung metastases from melanoma. It is known to control metastasis-associated protein kinase C (PKC) and SRC (Gene of Rous sarcoma virus) signaling through direct scaffolding activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of NSCLC is dependent on the tumor, node, metastasis (TNM) staging, and the performance status/comorbidities of the patient. Patients with poor performance status are associated with shortened survival. Poor appetite and weight loss are also poor prognostic signs. [18] [19] Lymphatic vessel invasion has a negative impact on prognosis, as does occult lymph node metastasis. Patients with actionable mutations have been shown to have a better prognosis. For example, activating EGFR mutations are found in adenocarcinoma associated with never smokers, women, and/or Asian ethnicity and generally have a significantly better prognosis. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology -- Intrinsic Cell Determinants
retrieved_docs.content: Colony-stimulating factor 1 (CSF1) acts as the mediator of lung metastases. It recruits macrophages, which, in turn, secrete epidermal growth factor. This is followed by CSF1 secretion by tumor cells and further recruitment of macrophages. The presence of macrophages indicates a highly invasive potential, which enhances the formation of neoplastic lung colonies.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Pleural Effusion -- Treatment / Management -- Malignant eosinophilic pleural effusion
retrieved_docs.content: MEPE’s pathogenesis involves 2 steps: accumulation and migration. Accumulation of eosinophils occurs due to increased production within the bone marrow. Migration is promoted by the firm cytoadherence of these eosinophils to endothelial cells. Increased production of cytokines such as interleukins 33, 4, and 5, which have been shown to have chemoattractant properties that aid in eosinophilic migration, has been reported in patients with non-small cell lung cancer. [220] Tumor-homing eosinophils are also shown to secrete chemokines, stimulating T-cell expansion within the tumor microenvironment. Eosinophils have been shown to enhance the maturation of dendritic cells in the tumor microenvironment, which overcomes tumor tolerance and has been associated with a better prognosis. [221] Though cancer-directed therapies can control pleural fluid formation in small-cell lung cancer, strategies that improve survival are still needed. Measures to palliate symptoms are similar to the ones employed for non-eosinophilic malignant pleural effusion. The percentage of eosinophils may represent an important marker of prognosis, and further research on this topic has been advocated.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology -- Intrinsic Cell Determinants
retrieved_docs.content: Successful metastasis formation requires early remodeling of the lung ECM in the metastatic niche. Tumor-related tenascin C  is an essential protein in the early phases of metastatic onset. There is a direct correlation between the expression of the extracellular matrix glycoprotein tenascin C and breast cancer metastasis to the lung. Tenascin C enhances the Wingless-related integration site (WNT) and NOTCH signaling pathway, critical in improving cancer cells' viability.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchoalveolar Cancer (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: However, the pathologic criteria for BAC remained somewhat nebulous. As knowledge of radiology, histology, and immunophenotyping continued to evolve, the necessity to identify certain subtypes among the conglomerate of NSCLC with a more favorable prognosis and less invasive treatment became increasingly important.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchoalveolar Cancer (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Consolidation to tumor ratio, defined as a ratio of the maximum diameter of the area of consolidation to the tumor, has also been used as a prognostic marker in non-solid adenocarcinoma with lepidic growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Tumor Biomarkers -- Interfering Factors -- Disadvantages of Tumor Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Lack of specificity: Some tumor biomarkers are produced by normal and cancerous cells and are elevated in many noncancerous conditions. This lack of specificity can lead to false-positive results, potentially leading to unnecessary diagnostic procedures or treatment interventions. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Lung -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry -- Histochemistry of the Lung
retrieved_docs.content: Immunohistochemistry and lectin histochemistry can provide substantial insights into obstructive and restrictive pathologies and lung cancer diagnosis. Adenocarcinomas can be identified using differentiation markers such as TTF-1 and NapsinA. Both these markers are expressed in more than 85% of the cases. [19] Metastasis can be confirmed by immunohistochemistry staining to identify the primary tumor tissue of origin. Distinct expression of CK7 and CK20 profiles, in addition to the absence of markers commonly expressed in primary lung cancer, can signify metastatic cancer. With the advent of cancer treatment using tyrosine kinase inhibitors, actionable gene mutations can be screened in patients with lung cancer. [20] EGFR mutation and ALK translocation are the most effectively targeted oncogenes in non–small cell carcinomas and are now considered standard treatment procedures. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The lung is one of the most common sites of cancer metastasis. This activity will provide an overview of the common cancers that metastasize to the lung, the diagnostic work-up, and currently available treatment options. It will also review the molecular basis of metastasis and the critical role of genetics, epigenetics, cytokines, and chemokines involved in the pathophysiology of metastasis. The importance of an interprofessional team to diagnosis and management will be reviewed.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In order to differentiate neuroendocrine lung tumors, INSM1 (Insulinoma-associated protein 1) is a transcriptional factor. It is inactivated by the HES1 (Hairy and Enhanced of Split-1) transcription factor. It promotes the expression of 3 neuroendocrine molecules: chromogranin A (CHGA), synaptophysin (SYP), and neural cell adhesion molecule 1 (NCAM1) via activation of transcription factors. INSM1 is emerging as a novel, sensitive, and specific IHC marker that may serve as a first-line marker of neuroendocrine differentiation. [2]

Melanomas of the skin are a member of this SMQ, a subtype of malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث), and through an inverse‑isa relationship are also linked to malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث), reflecting the overlap between cutaneous and oral‑cavity malignancies; clinically, they may be identified at the retina (ret) as a finding site, indicating possible ocular involvement or metastasis. The term “krk” is linked to the Czech designation nádory hlavy a krku (head‑and‑neck tumors), underscoring

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: specified sites, malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث), melanomas of skin (a subtype of malignant skin cancer that also encompasses malignant lip cancer), prostate adenocarcinoma, squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck, lung carcinoma of non‑smokers, and generic metastatic cancer. Melanomas of skin have been reported with a finding site in the retina, highlighting possible ocular involvement. Collectively, these hierarchical and inverse‑is‑a relationships create an integrated network that connects hereditary syndromes, primary organ‑specific cancers, and diverse metastatic presentations to the central concept of malignant tumors and secondary malignancies, supporting precise diagnosis, classification, and management of oncologic patients.

Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasms of intrathoracic lymph nodes (Secundaire en niet gespecificeerde maligne neoplasmata van intrathoracale lymfeklieren) are an inverse‑ISA of malignant tumor in the chest (ورم خبيث في الصدر), linking intrathoracic lymph‑node metastases to broader chest malignancies. The SMQ “non‑hematologic malignant diseases” also encompasses squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), lung carcinoma of non‑smoker cells (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة), metastatic squamous‑cell carcinoma, metastatic glandular carcinoma and a malignant tumor of the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان). The concept “cancer; other and unspecified primary” connects malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) with broader classes such as malignant chest tumor (ورم خبيث في الصدر) and generic malignant tumor (ورم صماعي خبيث); it is defined as not having the head‑and‑neck region (krk) as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية) is a specific form of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), as indicated by an inverse‑isa relationship, and its morphological features are directly associated with the metastatic squamous cell carcinoma entity (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). Both the primary squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and its metastatic lymph‑node counterpart, along with adenocarcinoma of the prostate (سرطان البروستاتا الغدي), lung carcinoma of non‑keratinizing cells (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة), and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية), are members of the non‑hematologic malignant diseases (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)). This grouping reflects that all these entities are solid malignant neoplasms outside the hematopoietic system, emphasizing their shared classification for clinical coding, research, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

Melanomas of the skin are characterized by a finding site of “ret,” are correctly placed under the parent category سرطان الجلد الخبيث (malignant skin cancer), and, despite an erroneous inverse relationship to malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث), are clinically classified as a subtype of malignant skin cancer. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas and thymic carcinoma are each classified as cancerous tumors and are included within the general categories of pancreatic cancer and malignant neoplasms, respectively. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a

Each of these metastatic entities is linked to a distinct morphological description: “Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck” is associated with metastatic glandular carcinoma (سرطانة غدية نقيلية); “Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck” is associated with metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي); and “Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck” is both a subtype of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and is associated with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary head‑and‑neck, prostate, and lung cancers are organized within the non‑hematologic malignant

Other entities listed as members of the non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ include سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة (lung carcinoma with non‑small cells) and the various metastatic and primary head‑and‑neck cancer forms described above, illustrating that the SMQ classification aggregates a wide spectrum of malignant neoplasms, both primary and metastatic, across different anatomical sites and histologic types.

Both metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck and squamous cell carcinoma of the trachea are classified within the non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة). The SMQ group encompasses a variety of tumors, including malignant tumor of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي), bone cancer (سرطان العظم) (which classifies the upper‑jaw tumor), lung carcinoma of non‑smokers, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. Through the “cancer; other respiratory and intrathoracic” category, the SMQ group connects to respiratory and intrathoracic malignancies. The SMQ is also a member of the melanomas of skin classification; melanomas of the skin are a distinct malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) associated with the finding site “ret,” and although they are linked in the relational data to malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة خبيث), they arise from melanocytes and represent a separate pathological entity. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 275

Metastatic squamous‑cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck is defined as an inverse “is‑a” of squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and possesses the morphology of metastatic squamous‑cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). Malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) is mapped from the broader concept of secondary malignant tumors in other specified sites, illustrating how organ‑specific malignancies derive from general secondary tumor classifications. Melanomas of skin are a form of malignant skin cancer and serve as a parent category of malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث). They are classified within the non‑hematologic malignant diseases (SMQ) group, which also includes metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي), squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), non‑small‑cell lung carcinoma (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: integrating primary sites (colon, stomach, upper jaw, prostate, lung, sublingual gland, retina), metastatic destinations (lymph nodes of the neck, head and neck, sublingual gland), morphological variants, and broader disease categories (secondary malignancies, non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ). This integrated framework supports accurate diagnosis, prognostication, and site‑specific therapeutic planning for patients with diverse malignant neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1118
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is identified as having the associated morphology of “سرطانة غدية نقيلية” (metastatic adenocarcinoma). Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck is a specific subtype of “سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق” (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck) and shares the associated morphology of “سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية” (metastatic squamous cell carcinoma). All of these entities—Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck, “سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق” (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck), “سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة” (lung carcinoma of non‑odor cells), “سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية” (metastatic squamous cell carcinoma), and “سرطانة غدية نقيلية” (metastatic adenocarcinoma)—are members of the broader classification “الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)” (non‑hematologic malignant diseases). This grouping underscores that these head‑and‑neck and lung malignancies, whether primary or metastatic, belong to the same non‑hematologic cancer spectrum, facilitating unified diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

In addition, metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck is an inverse isa of “سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق” (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). The “الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)” also includes other primary malignancies such as “سرطان البروستاتا الغدي” (glandular prostate cancer), “سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق” (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck), “سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة” (lung carcinoma of non‑odor‑producing cells), “سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية” (metastatic squamous cell carcinoma), and “سرطانة غدية نقيلية” (metastatic glandular carcinoma). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.
